title: Multithreading
content:
Multithreading is a process of executing multiple threads simultaneously.
Multithreaded programs are capable of doing more than one tasks in parallel.
Multithreading allows different parts of the program to execute simultaneously.
Applications that perform several tasks that are not dependent on one another benefit the most from multithreading.
Parallelism is about doing multiple tasks at once. Concurrency is about dealing with multiple tasks at once.
Multi-threaded program: multiple instructions are executed in parallel or atleast appear to be executed.

title: Thread
content:
A thread of execution is the smallest sequence of programmed instructions that can be managed independently by a OS scheduler. Threads of a process share its executable code and the values of its dynamically allocated variables and non-thread-local global variables at any given time. Local primitive variables are thread-safe because each thread has separate stack memory. Local reference variables are not thread-safe because each thread share heap memory. A thread is a sequence of instructions within a program that can be executed independently of other code. Thread is a dispatchable unit of work, includes a processor context (program counter and stack pointer) and its own data area for a stack. Each thread is executing the same program code and shares the same data area and heap however each thread has it own stack containing local variables.

Eahc thread has a priority, thread scheduler uses priority to determine which thread should be allowed to execute.
Threads with higher priority are executed in preference to threads with lower priority.
When two threads have the same priority then JVM will execute them in FIFO order.
When multiple threads are ready to execute then JVM will selects and executes the Runnable thread which has the highest priority.

title: Race condition
content:
Race condition occurs when multiple threads try to update mutable shared data at the same time.
Two or more threads simultaneously reading data from the same object is not a problem.
When many threads are simultaneously try to update the same object then memory inconsistencies may arise.
When one thread is updating the object while the other is reading it then memory inconsistencies may arise.
  • Read-read sharing is safe.
  • Both read-write and write-write races are a problem.
When multiple threads try to modify a shared resource at the same time then the final outcome is non deterministic.
We must explicitly synchronize concurrent access to a shared resource in a multi-threaded environment.
When a thread enters synchronized code (calling the synchronized method of that object) it takes the lock on that object.
Thus other threads cannot enters synchronized code because there is only one lock per object.
Cached shared data causes a thread not to notice that data has been changed by the another thread.
Resources: Memory, files, network connections.
Solutions to race condition:
  • Locks-Mutexes: Ensure only one thread can access the shared data at a time, like a key to a room.
  • Atomic Operations: Operations that complete indivisibly, preventing interruption.
  • Semaphores-Monitors: Other synchronization tools to control access to resources.
Examples
Bank Account Two people withdraw money from the same account at once. Without locks, both might read the balance, one withdraws, then the second withdraws, potentially leaving the account overdrawn or losing one withdrawal's record. Movie Ticket Suppose you are buying theatre tickets online and after you pick your seats and are entering your credit card information, someone else buys that seat and they are no longer available. Suppose multiple threads have access to a collection object and they are adding and removing at same time then there will be an error unless the collection is thread-safe. Document Editing Two users edit the same document. One saves changes, but before they're fully synced, the other saves, overwriting the first user's work. Shared Counter Two threads try to increment a counter variable.

title: Reactive
content:
Reactive programming is a declarative programming paradigm concerned with data streams and the propagation of change.
Reactive programming allows applications to react to updates automatically and efficiently.
Like reacting to something when user clicks this button run this function or when file IO operation is complete run this function. Imperative means when we are doing all the steps.
While declarative is where we are declaring this is what i want to happen and something is going to make that happen.
An imperative approach involves explicitly writing out the sequence of steps to achieve a result.
While a declarative approach describes the desired outcome and the programming language figures out the execution.
Main purpose of reactive programming is to avoid blocking in our code.

Synchronous = Sequential
Asynchronous = Non-sequential

List<Person> findByName(String name) -> void findByName(String name, Callback<List<Person>> callback)
BigDecimal getIncome(String name) -> void getIncome(String name, Callback<BigDecimal> callback)

title: Thread Pool
content:
Thread pool is a collection of pre-created worker threads that are kept ready to perform tasks.
Thread pool avoids the overhead of creating and destroying new threads for each task.
When a task is submitted, it is assigned to an available thread from the pool.
When all threads are busy, the task waits in a queue until a thread becomes free.
Thread pools improve performance by reusing threads and manage resources by limiting the no of threads running concurrently.

Key characteristics:
  • Worker threads: A set of threads that are created at the start and are always ready to execute tasks.
  • Task queue: A queue to hold tasks when all threads in the pool are busy.
  • Configurable: Thread pools allow configuration of the no. of threads, type of queue to use and how to handle rejected tasks.
How it works:
  • Task submission: Tasks are submitted to the thread pool, not directly to individual threads.
  • Task assignment: An available worker thread from the pool is assigned to the task.
  • Queueing: If all threads are busy, the task is placed in a waiting queue.
  • Thread reuse: After completing a task, the thread returns to the pool to wait for another task, instead of being terminated.
Advantages:
  • Improved performance: Reduces the significant cost of creating and destroying threads for each task.
  • Resource management: Limits the total no. of threads, preventing the system from running out of resources like memory.
  • Simplified programming: Developers can focus on the task logic rather than the complexities of managing thread lifecycles.

title: Monitor
content:
Synchronization is built around an internal entity known as the monitor.
Monitors are used to achieved mutual exclusion and threads cooperation.
A monitor is an entity that possesses a lock, a wait-queue and an entry-queue.
Every Java objects has a monitor associated with them.
Lock enforces mutual exclusive access to synchronized code block or method invoked on the associated object.
Entry-set is the set of all the threads who has already requested for the lock and the lock is not acquired by them yet.
Wait-set is the set of all the threads waiting on an monitor object that is used to provide coordination between multiple threads.

A lock is a tool for controlling access to a shared resource by multiple threads.
Every objects in java has an intrinsic lock associated with it.
Only one thread at a time can acquire the lock and all access to the shared resource requires that the lock be acquired first.
When a new thread tries to acquire the lock and if already a thread owns the lock then that thread will be waiting on the entry set to acquire the lock. When the thread which is acquired the lock completes execution of its critical section then it will release the lock. Next thread will acquire the lock but this next thread is taken from the entry set and will be determined by JVM based on some criteria like FIFO.

title: Mutual exclusion
content:
Mutual exclusion is a property of concurrency control, which is instituted for the purpose of preventing race conditions.
Race condition occures when a shared resource is accessed by different actors at the same time.
Both mutex and semaphore helps managing access to shared resources but they do it in different ways.
Mutex is suited for exclusive access whereas semaphore is suited for limited concurrency.
Mutual exclusion helps keep threads from interfering with each other while sharing data.
Mutual exclusion ensures that only one thread can have access to shared data at a time.
Mutual exclusion or atomicity ensures that write functionality is thread-safe.
Mutex is a program object which prevents simultaneous access to a shared data by multiple threads.
Critical section is region of the code where shared data between multiple threads is accessed.
Shared data is considered as a critical area.

Mutual exclusion can be achieved through:
  • Synchronized block
  • Synchronized method
  • Static synchronization

title: Deadlock
content:
A deadlock occurs when there is more than one thread, each waiting for a resource held by another.
Which occurs when multiple threads acquires the same set of lock in different order.
The most obvious kind of resource is an object monitor but any resource that causes blocking such as wait-notify can qualify.

Deadlock happens when two or more threads become interlocked:
  • Thread A holds the lock in object1 but is waiting on object2
  • Thread B holds the lock in object2 but is waiting on object1

title: Synchronization
content:
Synchronization is a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent processes or threads do not simultaneously execute some particular program segment known as critical section.Processes' access to critical section is controlled by using synchronization techniques. When one thread starts executing the critical section (serialized segment of the program) the other thread should wait until the first thread finishes. There is no any guarantee about exactly when a blocked thread is unblocked when the synchronized block is free again. This is typically up to the OS or execution platform to coordinate the unblocking of blocked threads. Thread synchronization is a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent threads do not execute some particular section of program especially critical section. There are two types of thread synchronization mutual exclusion and inter-thread communication.

Following are some classic problems of synchronization:
  • Producer-consumer problem
  • Readers-writers problem
  • Dining philosopher problem
Thread cooperation is achieved using the wait-set which is associated with the monitor and wait-notify mechanism.
Cooperation is important when one thread needs some data to be in a particular state and another thread is responsible for getting the data into that state.

When a thread invokes the wait method respect to an object then the thread will be suspended and added to the wait-set to wait until some other thread invokes notify or notifyAll on the same object. If a thread was put in the queue by a call to wait then it must be unfrozen by a call to notify before it can be scheduled for execution again.
Notify method is used for waking up threads that are in the wait-set of the monitor of a particular object.
A thread may call wait, notify or notifyAll on an object only if it owns the monitor lock of that object.

title: Synchronized
content:
A block of code marked with synchronized becomes a synchronized block.
Keyword synchronized enforces exclusive access to the block of code.
Only one thread is allowed to enter this block of code at the same time.
Thread that first enters the synchronized block remains in runnable state.
Synchronized blocks and synchronized methods are mutually exclusive.
Synchronized method can be static method or instance method.
The synchronized keyword does not support fairness.
Fairness property provides a lock to the longest waiting thread (contention).
Any thread can acquire lock once released, no preference can be specified.
All other threads accessing the same block of code enters the blocked state.
When a runnable thread exits the synchronized block then lock is released.
Another thread is now allowed to enter the runnable state and place a new lock.

When two or more threads modify an object then declare the methods that carry out the modification as synchronized.
When a thread wants to execute a synchronized method on a given object, first it has to acquire the lock of that object.
Once thread acquired the lock then it is allowed to execute any synchronized method on that object.
When a thread calls a synchronized method on an object then the JVM ckecks the monitor lock for that object.
When the monitor lock is unowned then ownership is assigned to the calling thread which is then allowed to proceed with call.
When the monitor lock is owned by another thread then the calling thread will be put on hold until the lock becomes available.
When a thread exits a synchronized method it releases the monitor lock, allowing a waiting thread to proceed.
When a class has one or more synchronized methods, each object of the class gets a queue that holds all threads waiting to execute one of the synchronized methods.
